The **Belgian Cup** (French: *Coupe de Belgique*;[[a]](#cite_note-1) Dutch: *Beker van België*;[[b]](#cite_note-2) German: *Belgischer Fußballpokal*[[c]](#cite_note-3)) is the main [knockout](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament") [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") competition in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), run by the [Royal Belgian FA](/wiki/Belgian_Football_Association "Belgian Football Association"). The competition started in 1908 with provincial selections as the "Belgian Provinces Cup". Starting from 1912 only actual clubs were allowed to partake. As of 1964, the Belgian Cup has been organised annually. Since the 2015–16 edition, the Belgian Cup is called the **Croky Cup**, for sponsorship purposes. The final traditionally takes place at the [Heysel Stadium](/wiki/Heysel_Stadium "Heysel Stadium") in Brussels.

The most successful cup club is [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV") with 12 Belgian Cups in their possession. The current champions are Club Brugge, having beaten [rivals](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht%E2%80%93Club_Brugge_KV_rivalry "R.S.C. Anderlecht–Club Brugge KV rivalry") [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") 2–1 in the [2025 final](/wiki/2025_Belgian_Cup_final "2025 Belgian Cup final"). The winners are awarded a challenge cup and qualify for the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") and the [Belgian Supercup](/wiki/Belgian_Super_Cup "Belgian Super Cup").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Michel_Preud%27homme_tijdens_zijn_succesverhaal_bij_KAA_Gent.jpg/330px-Michel_Preud%27homme_tijdens_zijn_succesverhaal_bij_KAA_Gent.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michel_Preud%27homme_tijdens_zijn_succesverhaal_bij_KAA_Gent.jpg)

[Michel Preud'homme](/wiki/Michel_Preud%27homme "Michel Preud'homme") holding the Belgian Cup after winning it with [KAA Gent](/wiki/K.A.A._Gent "K.A.A. Gent") in 2010.

### First national cup competitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First national cup competitions")]

The first cup competition ever in Belgium was held in 1907–08 but the teams were not actual teams but were provincial selections. The winner would be awarded a silver trophy by [prince Albert](/wiki/Albert_I_of_Belgium "Albert I of Belgium"). Football was especially played in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") and [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_Province "Antwerp Province"). In [West](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders") and [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders"), notably [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") and [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), football was also becoming more and more loved. In [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"), football was adored around the city of [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"). The province of [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders") won to that of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_(province) "Antwerp (province)") by 6–2. The next year, the province of Antwerp beat that of [Brabant](/wiki/Brabant_(province) "Brabant (province)") by 5–2. The cup was then suspended after two years.

In 1912, the Belgian Cup made a first return. This time only actual clubs from the two highest levels were allowed to partake. 16 clubs would fight for the Kings' Cup in a [knockout competition](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament"). [Racing Club de Bruxelles](/wiki/Racing_Club_de_Bruxelles "Racing Club de Bruxelles") won the first Belgian Cup for clubs, after beating [RC de Gand](/wiki/K.R.C._Gent "K.R.C. Gent") in the final. The competition would return the next year. This time, preliminary rounds would be organised so regional could partake as well. [Union Saint-Gilloise](/wiki/Royale_Union_Saint-Gilloise "Royale Union Saint-Gilloise") would go on and win this edition's final after extra time. Having already won the national league three weeks before, the team became the first [double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)") winners in Belgian football history. They would end up winning the cup the following year as well.

During the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), no organised association football was played in Belgium. It would take until 1926–27 for the Belgian Cup to make its comeback. t's also the first time the competition is officially named the Belgian Cup. This edition would be won by [R Cercle Sportif Brugeois](/wiki/Cercle_Brugge_K.S.V. "Cercle Brugge K.S.V."), the first [Flemish](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") team to achieve this. They also won the national league, which made them the second team to win the Belgian double. Unfortunately, the cup fell into disgrace among the leading clubs at the time. During the [interbellum](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period") the competition would only be organised one more time, in 1935–36. The 1944 edition was never finished.

In 1953, the Belgian Cup made another comeback. This edition of the cup would be won by [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège"), the first [Walloon](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") team to do so. Three years later, a poll was organized after which the Belgian cup was stopped once again.

### Gateway to Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Gateway to Europe")]

The foundation of the [European Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup") is the main reason an annual cup tournament was brought back to life in 1963-1964. The first four editions of this European Cup had no Belgian participants due to the absence of a national cup.

Since 1964, the Belgian Cup winner would be qualified for the European Cup Winners' Cup, until its discontinuation in 1999. From then on, the cupholders would get a place in the [UEFA cup](/wiki/UEFA_cup "UEFA cup"), which became the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") in ten years later. Since 2021, the winner will enter the European competition in the play-off round. Because teams have to play a fewer number of games compared to the national league to get a European ticket, the Belgian Cup was nicknamed 'The quickest way to Europe'. This meant the top teams grew more interest in the national cup, which increased the prestige of the competition. Due to all of this, the traditional big three of Belgian football ([FC Bruges](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV"), [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") and [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège")) would win the cup the most times. The most successful cup club is [Club Brugge KV](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV") with 12 cups followed by [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") with 9 and [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") with 8. Bruges would go on and win the double twice, in 1976–77 and 1995–96. Anderlecht has won one more, in 1964–65, 1971–72 and 1993–94. If the cup winner secures an equivalent or better (Champions League) European ticket through the league, the next available ticket in the league standings will be upgraded. In such a case, all subsequent tickets will also move up by one position.[[1]](#cite_note-4)

[KV Mechelen](/wiki/K.V._Mechelen "K.V. Mechelen") won the cup in 2019 as well as becoming champions in the [First Division B](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division_B "Belgian First Division B"), winning the *small double*. It was the second time a team from the second level won the cup and the first time it came with a European ticket. Due to being involved in a [fraud scandal](/wiki/2017%E2%80%932019_Belgian_football_fraud_scandal "2017–2019 Belgian football fraud scandal"), the club wasn't allowed to enter the European competitions for a year. Their European ticket was passed on to Standard Liège based on the standings in the [Jupiler Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division_A "Belgian First Division A"). All the lower tickets were passed on as well. This meant that, of all teams, losing finalists [KAA Gent](/wiki/K.A.A._Gent "K.A.A. Gent") got into the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") at the very last minute.[[2]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Cupfinal_Belgium_2018-19.jpg/250px-Cupfinal_Belgium_2018-19.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cupfinal_Belgium_2018-19.jpg)

Picture taken during the [2019 final](/wiki/2019_Belgian_Cup_Final "2019 Belgian Cup Final") between [Mechelen](/wiki/K.V._Mechelen "K.V. Mechelen") and [Ghent](/wiki/K.A.A._Gent "K.A.A. Gent").

### Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Venues")]

The cupfinal is traditionally played in and around the capital. The editions before WWII were played in [Jette](/wiki/Jette "Jette"), [Uccle](/wiki/Uccle "Uccle") and [Forest](/wiki/Forest,_Belgium "Forest, Belgium"). The three finals in the 1950s were played in the [Heysel Stadium](/wiki/Heysel_Stadium "Heysel Stadium") in Brussels. Since the competitions annual return in 1964, this stadium has hosted the final. The only exceptions were between 1985 and 1987 and 1992–1995. During these years, the final was played four times in the [Constant Vanden Stock Stadium](/wiki/Constant_Vanden_Stock_Stadium "Constant Vanden Stock Stadium") in [Anderlecht](/wiki/Anderlecht "Anderlecht"), twice in the [Olympiastadium](/wiki/Jan_Breydel_Stadium "Jan Breydel Stadium") in [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") and once in the [Stade Maurice Dufrasne](/wiki/Stade_Maurice_Dufrasne "Stade Maurice Dufrasne") in [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège").

### Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Trophy")]

The first design was used for the three editions in the 1950s. Since 1964, a second design has been given to the cup winner. The ceremony takes place solely on the pitch and not on the grandstand. The winning team are entitled to keep the trophy for 11 months, until it's returned to the [RBFA](/wiki/RBFA "RBFA"). This means the trophy is a real challenge cup and has gotten some bumps and scratches along the years.[[4]](#cite_note-7) Every player and member of staff of the winning side is awarded a medal. Until the 2022 final, they got a minitature version of the trophy. The referees and losing side receive medals.[[5]](#cite_note-8)

The current trophy features a silver [loving-cup](/wiki/Loving_cup "Loving cup") design. Ribbons in the victors' colors are tied along the ears. There's no real front or rear side, but a [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and French side. The Dutch side reads "Beker van België wisseltrofee aangeboden door dagblad Les Sports", the French side "Coupe de Belgique challenge offert par le journal Les Sports". Both statements have the same meaning and translate to "Belgian Cup challenge offered by newspaper Les Sports". Every victor since 1964 and the corresponding year are engraved on the cup as well.

### Naming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Naming")]

The first two editions of the Belgian Cup were played with provincial sides. During this time, the competition was called **Belgian Provinces Cup** (*Beker der Belgische Provincies* in Dutch, *Coupe des Provinces Belges* in French). The editions before [World War 1](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") were known as the **Kings' Cup** (*Beker van de Koning* in Dutch, *Coupe du Roi* in French). Every edition of the cup after 1918 was called the **Belgian Cup** (*Beker van België* in Dutch, *Coupe de Belgique* in French).

In the 1995–96 season, a sponsored name was first introduced. [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola") had bought the naming rights for five seasons, and named the competition the **Coca-Cola Cup**.[[6]](#cite_note-9) There was no sponsored name between 2001 and 2007. In January 2008, the Belgian FA announced [Cofidis](/wiki/Cofidis "Cofidis") had bought the naming rights for 300,000 euros.[[7]](#cite_note-10) Eight editions of the **Cofidis Cup** would be played. After Club Bruges won their 11th cup in 2015, [Croky](/wiki/Croky "Croky") became the new main sponsor of the tournament.[[8]](#cite_note-11) The Belgian cup is called the **Croky Cup** to date.

## Competition format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Competition format")]

### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Overview")]

Beginning in July or August, the competition proceeds as a [knockout tournament](/wiki/Knockout_tournament "Knockout tournament") throughout, consisting of eight rounds, a semi-final and then a final. All teams playing at the national level of football (Levels 1 through 5) are expected to participate, together with the top teams from the [Belgian Provincial Leagues](/wiki/Belgian_Provincial_Leagues "Belgian Provincial Leagues"). The provinces each receive a number of entries depending on their number of inhabitants. To determine which teams from each province can participate, each province can devise their own ruling, but commonly tickets are awarded to the best performing teams in each respective provincial cup tournament of the prior season, with any remaining tickets awarded to the highest finishing teams not already qualified in the highest provincial league. As a result, most teams from the Provincial Leagues participating in the Belgian Cup are playing in the top two provincial divisions, although each season a few teams from the lower divisions succeed in qualifying.

A system of [byes](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") ensures clubs above Level 5 enter the competition at later stages. In round 1 only, teams are grouped geographically prior to the draw to reduce travel costs for smaller teams. The clubs active in European football only enter the competition in round 7. Youth teams, active in level 4 to 2, are not allowed to compete. No [seeding](/wiki/Seed_(tennis) "Seed (tennis)") occurs.[[9]](#cite_note-12) In rounds five through seven, in case an amateur team (Level 3 or below) is drawn against a professional team (Level 2 or above), the amateur team will always receive home advantage if their ground meets the regulation specifications. The final is played at the [King Baudouin Stadium](/wiki/King_Baudouin_Stadium "King Baudouin Stadium").

In the first three rounds, fixtures ending in a tie are decided by penalty kicks immediately, extra time is only played from round four onwards and possibly followed by penalty kicks if necessary. The semi-final round is the only round played over two legs; as such, extra time and penalty kicks can only occur in the return match. The [away-goal-rule](/wiki/Away_goals_rule "Away goals rule") is not applied in the semi-finals.[[10]](#cite_note-13)

### Schedule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Schedule")]

Qualified entrants from the provincial leagues (levels 6 through 9) begin the competition in the first round together with teams from the [Belgian Third Amateur Division](/wiki/Belgian_Third_Amateur_Division "Belgian Third Amateur Division") (level 5). Clubs from higher levels are then added in later rounds, as per the table below. The months in which rounds are played are traditional, with exact dates subject to each year's calendar.

| Round | New entrants in this round | Month |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Preliminary round | Level 6 through 9 clubs[[11]](#cite_note-14) | July |
| **Main tournament** | | |
| First round | Level 5 through 9 clubs | August |
| Second round | Level 4 clubs |
| Third round | Level 3 clubs |
| Fourth round | *none* |
| Fifth round | Level 2 clubs | September |
| Round of 32 | Level 1 clubs | October |
| Round of 16 | Teams active in a European competition | November |
| Quarter-finals | *none* | December |
| Semi-finals | February or March (single leg) |
| Final | April |

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

### Domestic coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Domestic coverage")]

The broadcasting rights for the Croky Cup are held by [Eleven Sports](/wiki/Eleven_Sports "Eleven Sports"). Due to the RBFA's obligation that broadcast cup games are free to watch, Eleven doesn't air the matches on their own platforms. [DPG Media](/wiki/DPG_Media "DPG Media") (on [VTM 2](/wiki/VTM_2 "VTM 2")) selects and buys games to air on [Flemish](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") television and [RTL](/wiki/RTL_Group "RTL Group") (on [Club RTL](/wiki/Club_RTL "Club RTL")) does the same for the [French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium").[[12]](#cite_note-15)[[13]](#cite_note-16) DPG Media sells some games to [VRT](/wiki/VRT_(broadcaster) "VRT (broadcaster)"), who broadcast these games on [Sporza](/wiki/Sporza "Sporza"). The final is always broadcast on VTM 2 in Flanders.

Teams whose games haven't been chosen for broadcasting are allowed to broadcast these games themselves. Regional broadcasters often try to buy the broadcasting rights to games between sides from that region, if DPG Media or RTL didn't select those games.[[14]](#cite_note-17)

### International coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_Cup&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: International coverage")]

Eleven Sports holds the rights for the [First Division A](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division_A "Belgian First Division A"), [First Division B](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division_B "Belgian First Division B"), [Supercup](/wiki/Belgian_Super_Cup "Belgian Super Cup") and [Women's Super League](/wiki/Belgian_Women%27s_Super_League "Belgian Women's Super League") as well. This means that the international broadcasters are almost identical for the Belgian Cup.
